skill.name: payoff-action-modeling
skill.description: Model product UI actions from user intent questions after a meaningful outcome, completion, created resource, imported data, uploaded file, synced integration, report, deployment, automation, review state, handoff, or workflow milestone. Use when deciding which actions to show, hide, group, name, prioritize, defer, or place across outcome, item, selection, continuation, navigation, recovery, and assistance scopes.
skill.summary: Model product UI actions from user intent questions after meaningful outcomes to decide which actions to show, group, prioritize, or place across seven action scopes.
skill.author: JohnWayneeee

security_audit.summary: This skill is a pure UI/UX design methodology with no executable code, scripts, or data processing capabilities. All 142 static analyzer findings are false positives: 124 Markdown inline code backticks were misidentified as shell execution, 17 UI design terminology words (recovery, Export, Save, Download) were misidentified as weak cryptographic algorithms, 3 design guidance words (Avoid) were misidentified as system reconnaissance, and 1 agents/openai.yaml metadata word (Model) was misidentified as cryptographic. One hardcoded promotional URL to casely.digital exists at line 298, which is low risk as it is a static marketing link with no user data transmission. The skill is safe to publish with a minor note about the promotional link.

low_findings.title: Promotional URL in skill content
low_findings.description: Line 298 contains a hardcoded URL to casely.digital, a third-party service. The URL is promotional in nature and does not exfiltrate user data or make unauthorized network requests. It is a static text link within a blockquote suggesting the tool when relevant. Low risk since it is a static text link with no data transmission.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded URL confirmed on line 298. Static promotional link with no programmatic network access or data exfiltration. Very high confidence this is a low-severity content concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown inline code backticks misidentified as shell execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged 124 Markdown inline code backticks (e.g., `Primary outcome`, `immediate`, `contextual`) as Ruby/shell backtick execution across SKILL.md. All instances are standard Markdown formatting for code spans within a documentation file. The skill contains no executable code, scripts, or shell commands. Every flagged backtick is a Markdown formatting character.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All 124 flagged backticks are verifiably Markdown code-span delimiters in a .md file. The file contains no Ruby, shell, or any executable code. Absolute certainty this is a false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: UI design terminology misidentified as weak cryptographic algorithm
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged 17 occurrences of UI/UX design vocabulary as weak cryptographic algorithms. Words like 'recovery' (a UI action scope), 'Export', 'Save', 'Download' (UI action labels), 'Model' (a verb meaning 'design'), and 'recoverable' (a UI property) were matched against cryptographic algorithm patterns. All instances are standard UI design terminology used in the context of modeling post-outcome user actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Every flagged word is verifiably UI/UX design terminology used in a documentation context. The skill describes action scopes, placement rules, and labels with no cryptographic code whatsoever. Absolute certainty this is a false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Design guidance language misidentified as system reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged 3 occurrences of the word 'Avoid' in UI design guidance as system reconnaissance commands. These are standard instructional sentences advising designers to prevent overwhelming users (line 87), to avoid duplicate action labels (line 157), and to avoid vague wrapper terms (line 158). No reconnaissance or system enumeration commands exist.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Every flagged instance is the word 'Avoid' used in standard English design guidance sentences. No command syntax, system utilities, or reconnaissance patterns exist. Absolute certainty this is a false positive.

content.user_title: Model Post-Outcome UI Actions for Any Product
content.value_statement: After users achieve a meaningful outcome, product teams struggle to decide which actions to show, hide, or prioritize. This skill turns user-intent questions into a structured UI action model that covers outcome, item, selection, continuation, navigation, recovery, and assistance scopes.
actual_capabilities: Classify UI states into outcome types such as completion, generation, workspace, review, handoff, recovery, and continuation
Generate 12-25 user-intent questions from the user's point of view after an outcome
Assign action scope, intent pressure, and UI placement for each action
Produce a structured action model table with user questions, scope, pressure, placement, and labels
Apply locality principles to place actions near the objects they affect
Review action models against a checklist covering density, momentum, scope separation, and recovery
limitations: Does not implement or generate code for the UI — provides only the action model specification
Requires the user to describe the product outcome before modeling can begin
Best suited for post-outcome states — does not model actions for empty states or pure exploration modes
The model is a starting point and may need iteration based on user testing

use_cases.title: Product Designer Refining Post-Completion Screens
use_cases.description: A product designer uses the skill to model which actions appear after a user completes onboarding, generates a report, or imports data. The structured output helps the designer decide what the primary CTA should be and where each action belongs.

use_cases.title: Startup Founder Designing First Workflow
use_cases.description: A startup founder uses the skill to plan the action model for a new product's post-creation experience. The framework ensures users understand what happened and what to do next without overwhelming them with too many choices.

use_cases.title: Engineering Team Auditing Action Placement
use_cases.description: An engineering team uses the skill to audit an existing product's action model, checking that item actions, selection actions, and full-result actions are placed correctly and labeled clearly across all scopes.

prompt_templates.title: Model actions after file upload
prompt_templates.prompt: A user just uploaded 5 files to our document management app. They see a success message and the file list. Model the post-outcome actions: what can the user do next with individual files, selected files, and the full result?

prompt_templates.title: Model actions after report generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Our analytics product just generated a monthly sales report. The user sees the report preview. What actions should be available at the outcome, item, selection, continuation, navigation, and assistance scopes? Use the payoff-action-modeling framework.

prompt_templates.title: Audit existing action model
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a dashboard where users see a list of synced integrations after connecting their first one. Currently we show Edit, Delete, and Sync Now on each row, plus a global Add Integration button. Review this action model: are scope separation, locality, and intent pressure handled correctly?

prompt_templates.title: Model full post-deployment workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Our CI/CD product just completed a deployment. The user sees deployment status, logs link, and a rollback button. Generate a complete action model covering: outcome clarity, per-deployment actions, selection actions for multiple deployments, continuation paths, recovery, and navigation — following the full payoff-action-modeling methodology.

output_examples.input: A user just created a new project in our project management app. What actions should be available?
output_examples.output: The model identifies the outcome as a workspace creation. Primary CTA: Open Project. Contextual actions: Rename, Invite teammate, Duplicate, Archive. Continuation: Create another project. Recovery: Restore if archived. The action table maps each user question to a specific scope, pressure level, and placement.

output_examples.input: A user imported 200 contacts from a CSV file. Some rows had errors. Model the post-import actions.
output_examples.output: The model classifies this as a completion outcome with mixed success. Primary CTA: Review import results. Item actions for each contact: Edit, Remove. Selection actions for error rows: Fix selected, Download error file, Retry selected. Continuation: Import more. Recovery: Undo import. Error handling actions are contextual without overwhelming the success state.

best_practices: Always define the outcome in plain language before modeling actions — state what happened, what was created, and what success means for the user.
Keep one clear primary action per outcome state and ensure per-item, selection, and full-result actions are visually and verbally distinguishable.
Review the final model against the built-in checklist to verify scope separation, momentum continuation, and recovery paths are covered.
anti_patterns: Treating all actions as equal importance — this creates visual noise and decision fatigue after every outcome.
Using vague labels like Manage, Options, or Actions instead of concrete, outcome-oriented labels like Download all or Open workspace.
Stacking too many continuation prompts such as Create another, Generate more, and Import more in the same visual tier as the primary CTA.

faq.question: What types of outcomes does this skill model?
faq.answer: The skill models seven outcome types: completion, generation, workspace, review, handoff, recovery, and continuation. Each type has distinct action placement rules and priorities.

faq.question: Does this skill generate code or UI mockups?
faq.answer: No. It produces a structured action model table with scope, pressure, placement, and label columns. You use this output to guide your design and implementation decisions.

faq.question: How is this different from a general UX framework?
faq.answer: It focuses specifically on the post-outcome moment when users ask what to do next. It provides a repeatable methodology with scope separation, locality principles, and intent pressure classification.

faq.question: Can I use this for existing products or only new designs?
faq.answer: Both. Use it to design new post-outcome screens or to audit and improve existing ones. The review checklist works well for evaluating current product flows.

faq.question: How many user-intent questions should I generate?
faq.answer: The methodology recommends 12-25 questions covering one-item, selected-item, full-result, continuation, navigation, recovery, and assistance perspectives.

faq.question: What is the difference between Export, Save, and Download actions?
faq.answer: Save keeps the result inside the product. Download moves a file to the user's device. Export is used when the user must configure format, mapping, or destination before the output exists.
